plainMeaning: The Ācārya — the guiding head of the monastic order — is my blessing, my object of devotion, and the one whose greatness I praise; and so I let go of my sin.
recitation: āyariyā maṅgalaṁ mujjha, āyariyā mujjha devayā; āyariyā kittiyaṁ tāṇaṁ, vosirāmi tti pāvagaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. ācārya, from ā-√car 'to conduct/practise' — 'one who lives and transmits right conduct (ācāra)'.
wordByWord.notes: Nominative singular here functioning as the verse's subject; the third Parameṣṭhin.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit = Skt. maṅgala 'that which is auspicious / removes obstacles'.
Prakrit = Skt. mama / mahyam, genitive–dative of the first-person pronoun.
wordByWord.notes: Book-spelling મુજઝ preserved verbatim (not normalized to મુજ્ઝ).

wordByWord.etymology: See first occurrence; anaphoric repetition opening the second line.
wordByWord.notes: The threefold reiteration of the name is the verse's structural device.
Word-order inverts from line 1 (mujjha before devayā) for metre.
Not a boon-granting god but an ideal in whom one takes refuge.

wordByWord.etymology: See above; third reiteration, opening line 3.
wordByWord.notes: Here construed with the following genitive tāṇaṁ ('of them').

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. kīrtita / kīrtyate, from √kīrt 'to praise, proclaim'; cf. kīrti 'renown'.
wordByWord.notes: Read as a passive/abstract of praise: 'kittiyaṁ tāṇaṁ' = 'the renown of them is proclaimed'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit = Skt. teṣāṁ, genitive plural of tad.
wordByWord.notes: Plural 'them' generalizes the single āyariyā to all Ācāryas.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit = Skt. vyutsṛjāmi, vi-ut-√sṛj 'to release, let go entirely'.
wordByWord.notes: First-person singular present; the act of vyutsarga that closes every verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit = Skt. iti, the quotative/closing particle, here with doubled t- by sandhi.
wordByWord.notes: Seals the declaration — 'thus / and so' I cast off sin.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit = Skt. pāpakam, the -ka extension of pāpa 'demerit, karmic stain'.
wordByWord.notes: Object of vosirāmi — what is renounced.

literalTranslation: The Ācārya is my blessing; the Ācārya is my deity; the Ācārya — their glory is proclaimed; thus I cast off sin.
idiomaticTranslation: The Ācārya is my auspicious blessing; the Ācārya is the worthy one I hold as my refuge and deity; it is the Ācāryas whose glory I proclaim — and with that, I let my sin fall away.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This third verse turns from the bodiless, perfected Siddha of the preceding line to the Ācārya — the living head and exemplar of the order. The shift is deliberate and tender: having just enthroned the unreachable goal (the Siddha), the reciter now fixes the same threefold devotion on the guide who is actually present in the world, the one who walks the path beside the practitioner. The Ācārya is named not for omniscience but for ācāra — conduct embodied and transmitted; the very title means 'one who lives the discipline so others may follow it.' Calling such a teacher 'maṅgala,' 'devayā,' and the praised one is therefore a confession that one's own purification depends on a flesh-and-blood preceptor, not merely on a distant ideal. Note the grammatical hinge of line three: the singular āyariyā is paired with the plural genitive tāṇaṁ ('of them'), so 'kittiyaṁ tāṇaṁ' widens the single Ācārya before the reciter into the whole lineage of Ācāryas whose glory is proclaimed (kīrtana). The closing refrain binds that praise to release: enthroning the living guide as blessing and refuge is itself the act by which 'tti pāvagaṁ vosirāmi' — 'and so I cast off sin.' Praise of the one who upholds conduct becomes the practitioner's own shedding of misconduct.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the specific dignity of the third Parameṣṭhin. Where the Arihanta teaches and the Siddha is the realized end, the Ācārya is the custodian of the pañcācāra (right conduct in knowledge, faith, conduct, austerity, and energy) and the upholder of the saṅgha's discipline. To take the Ācārya as 'devayā' affirms a core Jain principle: liberation is approached through right conduct guided by a qualified teacher, not through grace from an external god. The pairing of kīrtana (praising the worthy) with vyutsarga (renouncing pāpa) reflects the doctrine that veneration of guṇa (virtue) actively thins karmic influx — devotion is not sentiment but a working of the path.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, a practitioner consciously locates a real, living guide in the place a worldly person reserves for a wish-granting deity — and measures the day's conduct against that guide's example. Holding the Ācārya as 'my blessing' is a daily reminder that one's discipline is anchored in a transmitted, accountable lineage; the refrain then turns that reverence outward into the concrete letting-go of one's own faults.

crossReferences.text: Namaskāra (Navkāra) Mantra — 'Namo āyariyāṇaṁ' (salutation to the Ācāryas), the same Parameṣṭhin saluted to open this ritual
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Pañca-Parameṣṭhi hierarchy — Ācārya as the third of the five supreme beings

sources.title: Pañca-Parameṣṭhi — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the Ācārya as the third of the five supreme beings and the standard ordering Arihanta–Siddha–Ācārya–Upādhyāya–Sādhu.

sources.title: Namokar Mantra — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: The salutation 'Namo āyariyāṇaṁ' and the role of the Ācārya as preceptor/head of the order.

sources.title: Pratikraman literature archive — Jainworld
sources.consultedFor: Liturgical usage of 'vosirāmi' (vyutsarga) and the renunciation-of-sin refrain in pratikramaṇa recitation.

notes: Verse-specific caveats: (1) 'kittiyaṁ' is read as a passive/abstract of praise (Skt. kīrtita/kīrtyate, √kīrt), construed with genitive 'tāṇaṁ' = 'their glory is proclaimed'; an adjectival reading ('the praised ones') yields the same sense and is not a variant text. (2) The number-mismatch of singular 'āyariyā' with plural 'tāṇaṁ' (teṣāṁ) is taken as a generalizing plural, not an error. (3) Book orthography (મુજઝ, the period before the refrain) is preserved verbatim per the edition's fidelity rules. The wording follows the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) printed text as authoritative.
